metadata.title: Claude, Grounded.
metadata.source: Research prompt: Claude + Gmail / Google Workspace (Bear Brown); support.claude.com Google Workspace connectors, verified 2026-07-18

beats.narration_text: Namaste — this is Liam, in for Bear. Claude can take total control of your calendar — create, move, and delete events on its own. But it cannot send a single email. Not one. Even though, when you connect it, Google's login screen literally asks for email-sending permission. Same connector, same grant, two completely different ceilings. That gap is the whole video — not the setup.

props.command: Connect Claude to Gmail and Google Calendar.
props.runningText: reading the Google Workspace connector docs…
output: Calendar: full read/write — create, move, delete events.
Gmail: read + draft only — Claude structurally CANNOT hit send.
Same OAuth grant. Two different capability ceilings.

beats.act: Act 1 — this one's the real deal
beats.narration_text: First, set expectations, because this connector is different from the messy ones. No locked-down API, no partner-approval maze, no shady session-cookie wrappers. Anthropic ships Gmail, Calendar, and Drive as real, native, first-party connectors — free, on every plan, individual accounts included. So the temptation is a flat five-minute setup video. Don't watch that video. The interesting thing isn't how to connect it. It's what it refuses to do once connected.

shot.new_visual_element: First-party badge; three native connectors light up

beats.narration_text: Here's the asymmetry, straight from Anthropic's own docs. Calendar is full read and write — Claude creates, updates, and deletes events, finds mutual availability across attendees, manages who's accepted, sets up recurring meetings. A complete assistant. Gmail is read and draft only. Claude can search your whole inbox, read any thread, write a perfectly formatted reply — and then it stops. The exact words: it 'cannot send emails on your behalf.' Every draft waits for you to click send in real Gmail. One connector gives Claude the keys to your calendar and a pen for your inbox — but never the stamp.

shot.new_visual_element: Calendar full-control vs Gmail draft-only ceiling

beats.narration_text: Now the part that fills comment sections. When you connect Gmail, Google's consent screen lists a send-email permission — and people panic. 'Wait, so it CAN send?' Here's why it's there and why it's fine. Google bundles Gmail access into broad permission scopes; the drafting capability Claude uses lives in the same scope Google labels around composing and sending. Claude requests the scope, but its actual tools never call send. The permission is the door Google hands out; Claude only ever walks through the 'draft' room. A scope granted is not a capability used.

shot.new_visual_element: OAuth consent scope granted vs tool actually called

beats.narration_text: The setup itself is genuinely two minutes. Click the plus in the chat input, open Connectors, and toggle on Gmail, Calendar, or Drive — each one independently, so you can connect only Calendar and leave your inbox untouched if you want. Sign in through Google's normal screen, grant the permissions, and you're done. Then just ask for something that needs the data — Claude figures out which connector to reach for on its own. Watch.

props.command: Find a 30-minute slot next week where Bear and I are both free, and put a hold on it.
output: Read both calendars · found mutual availability.
(No connector chosen by you — Claude picked Calendar automatically.)

beats.narration_text: That's Calendar doing real work — mutual availability, an event created, not just read. Drive goes further than old summaries admit: it's not search-and-read anymore. Claude uploads files, creates folders, views permissions, and saves its own generated output straight back to your Drive. And Gmail: it drafts the reply — tone, context, formatting, all there — and then hands it back to you, unsent, sitting in your drafts folder. Three services, three different ceilings, and every single action needs your explicit approval in the chat. There is no background access. Claude never touches any of this while you sleep.

shot.new_visual_element: Three-connector capability tour: Calendar / Drive / Gmail ceilings

beats.narration_text: Now the single most common 'why isn't this working' moment — and it's not your fault. On a work Google account, you'll often hit a wall: 'Access blocked — your institution's admin needs to review Claude.' The fix isn't in Claude's settings at all. It's in the Google Admin console. An admin goes to admin dot google dot com, into Security, API controls, third-party app access, adds the Claude app, and marks it Trusted. Wait about fifteen minutes for the policy to propagate, reconnect, done. On Team and Enterprise, there's a layer before that too: an Owner has to switch these connectors on org-wide before anyone can even authenticate.

shot.new_visual_element: Access-blocked wall → Admin console trusted-app fix

beats.narration_text: The privacy point, stated precisely — because this is exactly where videos get called out. Anthropic's rule: your raw connector data is not used for training. Direct quote — 'We do not train our models on your Gmail, Drive, or Calendar connector data.' But here's the fine print that matters. If you copy content out of an email and paste it into the chat yourself, that pasted text is no longer 'connector data' — it's just your message, and on consumer plans where you've opted into training, it follows your normal training rules. The connector is walled off. Your own copy-paste is not. On Team and Enterprise, inputs aren't used for training by default anyway. Don't flatten this to 'your email is never used, period' — that's the version that's wrong.

shot.new_visual_element: Connector data walled off vs copy-pasted text following plan rules

props.sparkLine: The wall has a copy-paste gap.

beats.narration_text: And the honest limits, so you're not surprised. Gmail gives Claude attachment metadata but not attachment content — it knows a PDF is attached, it can't read inside it. Some advanced Gmail search filters aren't supported. Complex questions can take several API calls under the hood, so a big-inbox query runs a little slow — and heavy use bumps into Google's own rate limits. Drive extracts text only; images inside a document aren't processed, and Claude can't see Doc comments or suggestions. None of this breaks the tool. It just tells you where the edges are.

shot.new_visual_element: Known-limits card: attachments, filters, rate limits, text-only

beats.narration_text: Let's recap with Claude. The Google Workspace connectors are first-party, free, and on every plan — but they are not uniform. Calendar is a full read-write assistant: create, move, delete, coordinate. Drive goes well beyond search — uploads, folders, permissions, saving Claude's own output back. Gmail reads, searches, and drafts with real context — and structurally cannot send; every draft waits for your click. The consent screen asks for a send scope Claude never exercises — granted is not used. On work accounts, an admin marks Claude Trusted first. And raw connector data isn't trained on — but text you copy-paste into the chat follows your plan's ordinary rules. Powerful, native, and honestly bounded.

props.artifactHeading: Claude + Google Workspace — the honest map
artifactLines: Calendar: full read/write — create, update, delete events; find mutual availability; manage attendees.
Gmail: read + draft only. Structurally CANNOT send — every draft waits for your click in real Gmail.
Drive: beyond search — upload, create folders, view permissions, save Claude's own output back. Text-only.
Consent screen shows a send scope Claude never uses (granted ≠ used). Work accounts: admin marks Claude 'Trusted' first.
Raw connector data isn't trained on — but text you copy-paste into chat follows your plan's normal rules.

beats.narration_text: Your turn. Here's the prompt — read it with me, because connector details shift month to month and you should never trust a video, including this one, over the live docs. 'I'm connecting Claude to Gmail, Calendar, and Drive. Before anything, verify current details against support dot claude dot com rather than general knowledge. Confirm with sources: is Gmail still read-and-draft-only with no send? Is Calendar still full read-write? What's the current admin-enable requirement for work accounts, and the exact wording on training-data handling for connector data versus content I copy-paste in? Flag anything that's shifted.' That last line is the point — you're asking Claude to fact-check its own capabilities against the source of truth. Run it before you script, before you record, before you trust me. Liam, in for Bear.

props.command: I'm connecting Claude to Gmail, Calendar, and Drive. Verify current details against support.claude.com, not general knowledge. Confirm with sources: is Gmail still read-and-draft-only with no send? Is Calendar still full read/write? Current admin-enable requirement for work accounts? Exact wording on training-data handling for connector data vs content I copy-paste in? Flag anything that's shifted from what I expect.

beats.narration_text: Claude, Grounded. Liam, in for Bear.
